topic: An overview of programming paradigms, specifically comparing Imperative, Event-Driven, and Object-Oriented Programming to provide a mental framework for using classes.

concepts.name: Imperative Programming
concepts.explanation: Writing code as a specific, linear sequence of instructions that the computer follows from top to bottom (e.g., setting a variable, then drawing an ellipse).
concepts.why_it_matters: It is the foundational way of writing code, but it becomes difficult to manage as programs grow in complexity.

concepts.name: Event-Driven Programming
concepts.explanation: A style where the standard top-to-bottom control flow is interrupted by external actions, like a mouse click, which changes the direction of the program.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows programs to be reactive and interactive rather than just running a fixed script.

concepts.name: Object-Oriented Programming (OOP)
concepts.explanation: A paradigm centered around 'Classes' which act as blueprints or models for objects.
concepts.why_it_matters: It allows programmers to move away from managing individual lines of code and instead build scalable, reusable components.

concepts.name: Classes (as Blueprints)
concepts.explanation: A template that defines what an object is (its properties) and what it can do (its methods).
concepts.why_it_matters: Classes allow you to create complex systems by defining a single model and then creating many objects from it.

concepts.name: Properties and Methods
concepts.explanation: Properties are the characteristics of an object (like a vehicle's color or brand), and methods are the actions it can perform (like accelerating or braking).
concepts.why_it_matters: Understanding this distinction is essential for defining how objects behave in a digital environment.

code_examples.demonstrates: The top-to-bottom linear execution of imperative programming.

mistakes_and_fixes.symptom: The code is getting too long and hard to manage
mistakes_and_fixes.cause: Relying solely on imperative programming for complex systems.
mistakes_and_fixes.fix: Transition to Object-Oriented Programming using classes to create modular components.

key_moments.description: The instructor pivots from reviewing previous methods (functions/objects) to introducing the core concept of the module: Classes and OOP.
The instructor explains that OOP 'releases us from small things,' meaning it allows the programmer to focus on high-level architecture rather than individual lines of code.

student_questions: What is the difference between imperative and object-oriented programming?
Why do I need to use classes instead of just writing functions?
How do I make my code more organized and scalable?
What are properties and methods in a class?
Is JavaScript only one type of programming style?
How do I stop writing code line-by-line and start building components?
What does it mean when my code is 'interrupted' by a mouse click?
